q: Angle Class II molar relationship is defined by which molar position?
options: Mandibular first molar mesial to maxillary first molar
Only canine Class II with Class I molars always
Mandibular first molar distal to maxillary first molar (mesiobuccal cusp of U6 ahead of buccal groove of L6)
explanation: Class II: mandibular molar is distal relative to maxillary molar (Angle).

q: Angle Class III molar relationship means:
options: Only deep bite with Class I molars
Mandibular first molar mesial to maxillary first molar
Mandibular molar distal to maxillary molar
explanation: Class III: mandibular molar is mesial (prognathic tendency / mesial step extreme).

q: Angle Class II division 2 typically shows which incisor pattern?
options: Proclined upper incisors with large overjet only
Edge-to-edge anterior only with Class III molars
Retroclined maxillary central incisors with deep bite and often reduced overjet
explanation: II/2: retroclined U1s, deep overbite, often crowded laterals; overjet reduced vs II/1.

q: Angle Class II division 1 classic feature?
options: Retroclined centrals with deep bite only
Proclined maxillary incisors with increased overjet
explanation: II/1: proclined uppers, increased OJ, often incompetent lips.

q: In Angle Class I, the mesiobuccal cusp of the maxillary first molar:
options: Occludes distal to the mandibular second molar always
Is completely out of occlusion always
Occludes in the buccal groove of the mandibular first molar
explanation: Classic Angle Class I molar key.

q: A photo shows Class II molars, retroclined upper centrals, and deep bite. Best Angle label?
explanation: Molars Class II + retroclined centrals + deep bite = II/2.

q: Pseudo-Class III is most often due to:
options: Functional anterior shift / premature contacts guiding mandible forward
Only tongue thrust without occlusion role
explanation: Pseudo-Class III: CO–CR discrepancy from occlusal interferences; not true skeletal III.

q: Adult severe skeletal Class III with completed growth — common definitive pathway?
options: Only extraction of lower first molars always
Combined orthodontics and orthognathic surgery when discrepancy is skeletal and large
explanation: Large skeletal III in adults often needs ortho + surgery (e.g., BSSO / maxillary advancement).

q: Overjet measures the horizontal relationship of:
options: Maxillary to mandibular incisors in the anteroposterior direction
Canine cusp tip to midline only
explanation: Overjet = horizontal; overbite = vertical.

q: Overbite refers to:
options: Vertical overlap of maxillary over mandibular incisors
explanation: Overbite is vertical overlap.

q: 11-year-old with maxillary constriction needing skeletal expansion — appliance often chosen?
options: Haas or Hyrax-type rapid palatal expander (age-dependent)
explanation: Maxillary constriction → RPE (Haas/Hyrax) while midpalatal suture still responsive.

q: Haas expander differs from Hyrax mainly by:
options: Haas is a fixed retainer only
Haas is only for adults after SARPE always
Acrylic palatal coverage contacting mucosa (Haas) vs metal framework only (Hyrax)
explanation: Haas has acrylic pads; Hyrax is all-metal screw framework.

q: Quad helix is typically used for:
options: Slow maxillary expansion / molar derotation in mixed dentition
Only Class III reverse pull without expansion
explanation: Quad helix: slow expansion, arch development, molar rotation.

q: Adult with fused midpalatal suture needing significant transverse expansion — often requires:
options: Only lower expansion with quad helix
Surgically assisted RPE (SARPE) or segmental surgery pathways
explanation: After suture fusion, pure tooth-borne RPE risks dental tipping; SARPE often indicated.

q: Headgear in Class II growing patients is primarily used to:
options: Restrict maxillary forward growth / distalize upper molars (type-dependent)
explanation: Extraoral force to maxilla/molars modifies Class II growth pattern.

q: Facemask / reverse-pull headgear is classically for:
options: Only open bite tongue crib alone
Growing Class III with maxillary deficiency
Only adult skeletal Class III without growth
Only Class II division 2 deep bite
explanation: Protracts maxilla in growing maxillary-deficient Class III.

q: Functional appliances (e.g., Twin Block, Activator) aim mainly to:
options: Replace all fixed appliances in adults always
Redirect growth / posture mandible forward in growing Class II
explanation: Growth modification for Class II with favorable remaining growth.

q: Chin cup therapy historically targets:
options: Growing mandibular prognathism (Class III) — limited/controversial results
Only open bite without Class III
explanation: Chin cup attempts to restrain mandibular growth; evidence mixed.

q: Transverse discrepancy with unilateral posterior crossbite and functional shift — first priority often:
options: Extract all first premolars first always
Wait until all permanent teeth erupt always
Eliminate shift by expanding / correcting crossbite early
explanation: Functional shifts risk asymmetric growth; early crossbite correction preferred.

q: Lingual arch space maintainer primarily preserves:
options: Mandibular arch length / leeway space when primary molars lost
Only vertical dimension of occlusion in complete denture
explanation: Lower lingual arch holds molars and preserves E-space.

q: Band and loop space maintainer is indicated when:
options: A single primary molar is lost early and permanent successor not ready
Only for missing permanent central always
Only after full fixed ortho completion
All four first permanent molars are missing bilaterally always
explanation: Unilateral single-tooth primary molar loss → band & loop classic.

q: Distal shoe space maintainer is specifically considered when:
options: Primary second molar lost before eruption of first permanent molar
Only primary canine lost with adequate space
explanation: Distal shoe guides unerupted 6 into position when E lost early.

q: Nance appliance is used mainly to:
options: Maintain maxillary arch length / prevent molar mesial drift
explanation: Nance: acrylic button + bands on U6s — maxillary space maintainer.

q: Leeway space (E-space) is clinically important because:
options: There is no MD difference primary vs permanent
Primary molars are wider MD than premolars — can be used to resolve mild crowding
Permanent molars are always smaller than primary canines
explanation: E-space management can relieve mild crowding without extractions.

q: Premature loss of primary canine often results in:
options: Midline shift toward the loss side and crowding of permanent successor
Only increased overjet without midline change
explanation: Early C loss → midline deviation + space loss common.

q: Space maintainer is NOT needed if:
options: Primary second molar lost at age 4 with unerupted 6 always skip maintainer
Successor is about to erupt / space is already closed by design and no drift risk
Always skip if parent prefers no appliance
explanation: Indication depends on timing of successor eruption and drift risk.

q: Serial extraction concept is mainly for:
options: Only adult cosmetic alignment without crowding
Severe tooth-size/arch-length discrepancy guided extraction sequence in mixed dentition
explanation: Planned C → D → 4 sequence in carefully selected crowded mixed dentition.

q: Stainless steel crown (SSC) on a primary molar is classically indicated for:
options: Multi-surface caries or after pulp therapy
explanation: SSC restores multi-surface primary molars and post-pulpotomy/pulpectomy teeth.

q: Hall technique SSC differs because it typically:
options: Seals caries with SSC without traditional tooth preparation / LA in selected cases
explanation: Hall: cement SSC over carious primary molar without local prep in suitable cases.

q: Formocresol pulpotomy in primary molars is being replaced/supplemented by materials such as:
options: MTA or other bioceramic/ferric sulfate protocols (evidence-based alternatives)
Only pure eugenol forever as gold standard only
explanation: Modern pedo pulp therapy favors MTA/bioceramics over classic formocresol where available.

q: Primary molar with irreversible pulpitis and restorable crown — often:
options: Pulpectomy (or extraction if unrestorable / abscess pathway case-based) then SSC
explanation: Irreversible pulpitis/necrosis in restorable primary → pulpectomy + SSC often.

q: Indirect pulp treatment in deep primary caries aims to:
options: Leave deepest affected dentin, seal with biocompatible liner/restoration to avoid pulp exposure
explanation: IPT preserves vitality by sealing residual caries away from oral environment.

q: Most appropriate restoration after primary molar pulpotomy if multi-surface involvement?
options: Class I amalgam only without coverage
explanation: Full coverage SSC protects after pulp therapy.

q: Early childhood caries (ECC) is strongly associated with:
options: Only genetic enamel defects without diet role
Frequent sugar/bottle exposure especially night feeding with sweetened liquids
explanation: ECC: dietary sugars + bottle habits + plaque; maxillary anteriors classic.

q: Fluoride varnish is used in children primarily for:
options: Caries prevention / remineralization of early lesions
explanation: Professional fluoride varnish reduces caries risk.

q: If community water is optimally fluoridated and child is low caries risk, systemic fluoride supplements are:
explanation: Supplements only if water fluoride deficient AND risk warrants — avoid fluorosis.

q: Recommended amount of toothpaste for a toddler (smear/rice-size concept) aims to:
options: Provide topical fluoride benefit while limiting swallow/fluorosis risk
Use adult strip full length always
explanation: Age-appropriate pea/smear amounts reduce ingestion fluorosis risk.

q: APF gel ~1.23% fluoride professional application — key precaution?
options: Protect soft tissues / suction; avoid in patients who cannot control swallow well
Have child swallow fully for systemic benefit
explanation: High-concentration professional fluoride: minimize ingestion.

q: Pit and fissure sealants are most indicated for:
options: Deep pits/fissures in caries-risk permanent molars shortly after eruption
Only after full caries destruction of cusp
explanation: Sealants protect susceptible occlusal anatomy in at-risk teeth.

q: Tell-Show-Do is primarily a:
explanation: Tell-Show-Do: explain → demonstrate → perform; first-line behavior tool.

q: VOICE control in pedo behavior management means:
options: Controlled change in tone/volume to gain attention — not yelling abuse
explanation: Voice control is assertive communication, ethically applied with parent awareness.

q: Protective stabilization in pediatric dentistry requires:
options: No consent if procedure is short
Informed consent, indication documentation, and least restrictive approach
explanation: Protective stabilization is regulated ethically — consent + necessity.

q: Nitrous oxide in children is typically used for:
options: Mild–moderate anxiety with rapid onset/recovery when indicated and consented
explanation: N2O/O2 anxiolysis adjunct — not a substitute for LA or proper diagnosis.

q: Frankl behavior rating scale is used to:
options: Classify child cooperative behavior in dental setting
explanation: Frankl 1–4: definitely negative → definitely positive.

q: Pierre Robin sequence classic triad includes:
options: Hypertelorism, syndactyly, and Class II only
Micrognathia, glossoptosis, and cleft palate (airway concern)
Only natal teeth without jaw findings
explanation: PRS: small mandible → tongue back → cleft/airway issues.

q: Natal tooth vs neonatal tooth:
options: Natal present at birth; neonatal erupts within first 30 days
Natal always extracted day 1 without exam
Both terms mean permanent first molar
explanation: Timing defines natal vs neonatal; manage case-based (Riga-Fede, mobility, feeding).

q: Indication to extract a highly mobile natal tooth often includes:
options: Only extract if Class III molars
Always extract every natal tooth without assessment
Never extract even if aspirating risk
Aspiration risk / severe mobility / traumatic ulceration (Riga-Fede) interfering with feeding
explanation: Risk–benefit: mobility/ulceration/feeding issues drive extraction.

q: Ectopic eruption of permanent first molar commonly affects:
options: Maxillary first permanent molar resorbing distal of primary second molar
explanation: Ectopic U6 may resorb E distal; monitor/intercept.

q: Mesiodens most commonly located:
options: Mandibular buccal to first molar always
explanation: Mesiodens = most common supernumerary; midline maxilla.

q: Diastema with thick low frenum — sequence often taught:
options: Frenectomy first always before any ortho
Close space orthodontically first, then frenectomy if still indicated
explanation: Often align/close then revise frenum to reduce reopening risk (case-based).

q: Thumb sucking beyond early childhood may cause:
options: Only enamel hypoplasia of all teeth
Anterior open bite, increased overjet, and narrow maxilla patterns
explanation: Non-nutritive sucking habits reshape developing occlusion.

q: Tongue thrust / forward tongue posture is associated with:
options: Only deep bite Class II/2 always
Anterior open bite tendencies and speech/occlusion interactions
explanation: Habit/soft tissue patterns can maintain open bite.

q: Ankylosed primary molar radiographic/clinical clue?
options: Submerged below occlusal plane / infraocclusion and lack of mobility with metallic sound sometimes
explanation: Ankylosis → infraocclusion; may need extraction/space management.

q: Avulsed permanent tooth best immediate storage medium among common options?
options: Milk (or HBSS if available); keep moist — not dry
Tap water for days as best
explanation: Extraoral dry time critical; milk/HBSS preferred common media.

q: Intrusive luxation of immature permanent incisor — concern includes:
options: Pulp necrosis / obliteration risk and periodontal damage; monitor vitality closely
Always immediate root canal same day always for open apex
explanation: Intrusion is severe; open apex may re-erupt/monitor; closed apex different pathway.

q: Primary tooth intrusion — important rule:
options: Avoid damaging permanent successor; careful monitoring / do not aggressively replant primary usually
Always RCT primary same day always
explanation: Primary trauma: protect permanent tooth germ.

q: Most common site for supernumerary teeth?
options: Only third molar area never anterior
explanation: Maxillary midline most common.

q: Hypodontia most frequently involves which permanent teeth (excluding third molars)?
options: Mandibular second premolars and maxillary lateral incisors
explanation: Common missing: lower 5s, upper 2s (and third molars).

q: Taurodontism is:
options: Fusion of two tooth buds only
Elongated pulp chamber with apically displaced furcation
explanation: Bull-like tooth: large chamber, short roots relative.

q: Dens invaginatus (dens in dente) risk:
options: Early pulp involvement / caries in invagination — often maxillary laterals
Only periodontal abscess without pulp risk
explanation: Invagination can communicate with pulp; seal/prevent early.

q: Cervical vertebral maturation (CVM) or hand-wrist films are used to:
options: Assess skeletal growth stage for timing of growth modification
explanation: Growth staging guides functional appliance / facemask timing.

q: Bolton analysis evaluates:
options: Tooth size discrepancy between maxillary and mandibular arches
explanation: Bolton: MD tooth widths upper vs lower for ideal occlusion.

q: ANB angle approximately indicates:
options: Sagittal relationship of maxilla to mandible (jaw discrepancy)
explanation: ANB from SNA–SNB reflects AP jaw relationship (with caveats).

q: Curve of Spee leveling in ortho typically:
explanation: Deep curve of Spee correction consumes arch perimeter.

q: Retention after orthodontics is needed because:
options: Periodontal fibers and soft tissues tend to relapse toward original positions
Only third molars cause all relapse always
explanation: Relapse risk → retainers.

q: Hawley retainer advantages include:
options: Only bonded permanently to every tooth always
Adjustability and durability; allows some settling
explanation: Hawley: acrylic + wire; adjustable; classic removable retainer.

q: Fixed bonded retainer is often used for:
options: Lower anterior irregularity prevention after alignment
Only space maintenance for unerupted 6 with distal shoe role
explanation: 3–3 bonded wire common for mandibular anterior relapse control.

q: Root resorption risk during ortho increases with:
options: Only light forces for 2 weeks always
Heavy forces, prolonged treatment, pre-existing root anomalies, trauma history
explanation: Iatrogenic root resorption multifactorial — force magnitude/duration matter.

q: Ideal orthodontic force is generally:
options: Light continuous force producing efficient movement with less damage
explanation: Light continuous forces preferred biologically.

q: Center of resistance of a single-rooted tooth is approximately:
options: Along the root (roughly mid-root region conceptually)
In the pulp chamber crown only
At the adjacent contact point only
explanation: Force through center of resistance → translation concepts.

q: Tipping vs bodily movement:
options: Bodily movement always easier than tipping
Tipping needs less force/moment control; bodily movement requires controlled couple
explanation: Mechanics: moments and forces determine movement type.

q: Corticotomy-assisted ortho claims aim to:
options: Accelerate tooth movement via regional acceleratory phenomena (case-selected)
explanation: PAOO/corticotomy: selective surgery + ortho; not routine for all.

q: Interceptive ortho for anterior crossbite in mixed dentition often uses:
options: Removable appliance with Z-spring / inclined plane / limited fixed 2×4
explanation: Early correct dental anterior crossbite to avoid wear/shift.

q: Posterior crossbite in primary/mixed dentition with shift should be:
options: Corrected early when indicated to remove functional shift
explanation: Early transverse correction when functional shift present.

q: Primate spaces are normally found:
options: Mesial to maxillary canines and distal to mandibular canines in primary dentition
explanation: Primate spaces facilitate permanent incisor eruption alignment.

q: Ugly duckling stage refers to:
options: Transient maxillary midline diastema and flared laterals during mixed dentition before canine eruption
explanation: Physiologic diastema often closes as canines erupt — don't rush unnecessary treatment.

q: Early loss of primary second molar without maintainer often causes:
options: Distal drift of centrals only always
Mesial drift of first permanent molar and space loss for premolar
explanation: E loss → 6 drifts mesially → blocked-out premolar.

q: Primary teeth compared with permanent: enamel and dentin are generally:
options: Thicker enamel always with tiny pulps
Thinner enamel/dentin with larger pulp chambers relatively
explanation: Primary: thinner hard tissues, larger pulps → faster pulp involvement.

q: Strip crown technique is typically for:
options: Esthetic restoration of carious primary incisors
explanation: Celluloid strip crowns for primary anterior caries.

q: Mineral trioxide aggregate (MTA) advantage in pedo pulp therapy includes:
options: Only as temporary cement for SSC always
Biocompatibility and sealing with high success in pulpotomy/apexification contexts
Antibiotic properties equal to systemic amoxicillin
High tooth discoloration never an issue for all formulations
explanation: MTA/bioceramics favored for pulp and apex barriers; gray MTA can discolor.

q: Apexogenesis is performed when:
options: Vital pulp in immature permanent tooth — allow continued root development
Necrotic immature tooth only as first term
explanation: Apexogenesis = vital pulp therapy immature; apexification = necrotic immature barrier.

q: Most cariogenic sugar among dietary sugars commonly cited?
explanation: Sucrose strongly linked to caries; xylitol non-cariogenic polyol.

q: Xylitol chewing gum benefit mechanism mainly:
options: Non-fermentable sweetener reducing S. mutans / stimulating saliva
explanation: Xylitol: anti-caries adjunct, not sole hygiene.

q: Critical pH below which enamel demineralization accelerates is approximately:
explanation: Enamel critical pH ~5.5; dentin higher (~6.2) — know concept.

q: CAMBRA / caries risk assessment influences:
options: Frequency of recall, fluoride intensity, and sealant decisions
explanation: Risk-based prevention is modern pedo/operative standard.

q: First permanent tooth to erupt usually:
options: Maxillary canine at age 6 always first
Mandibular first permanent molar (around 6 years) — often with lower central
explanation: 6-year molar commonly first permanent (with lower centrals timing).

q: Mixed dentition period roughly spans:
options: From eruption of first permanent teeth to exfoliation of last primary (~6–12 years)
explanation: Mixed dentition = primary + permanent together.

q: Incisor liability refers to:
options: Only molar leeway space identical concept without incisors
MD width difference: permanent incisors larger than primary incisors — space challenge
explanation: Incisor liability: permanent incisors need more space than primary predecessors.

q: Flush terminal plane in primary molars often leads to:
options: End-on permanent molars initially; may convert to Class I with leeway space
explanation: Primary molar terminal plane predicts permanent molar relationship.

q: Mesial step primary molar terminal plane often predicts:
explanation: Mesial step → favorable Class I tendency.

q: Distal step primary terminal plane often predicts:
options: Class II permanent molar relationship tendency
Always ideal Class I without exception
explanation: Distal step → Class II risk.

q: Growth modification for Class II is most effective:
explanation: Time functional appliances to peak growth.

q: Skeletal open bite tendencies may involve:
options: Increased lower face height / steep mandibular plane patterns
explanation: Vertical growth patterns contribute to skeletal open bite.

q: Anterior open bite dental habit-related first approach often:
options: Habit cessation counseling / habit-breaking appliance as indicated
explanation: Eliminate habit before or with orthodontic correction.

q: Goshgarian transpalatal arch can:
options: Stabilize molars, control rotation, minor expansion/constriction
Replace lower lingual arch for mandibular E-space only
explanation: TPA: maxillary molar control.

q: Rapid palatal expansion midpalatal suture opening clinical sign?
options: Only root resorption of all teeth same day
Appearance of midline diastema between central incisors
explanation: Midline diastema often appears during active RPE — suture opening sign.

q: Retention of RPE result often uses:
options: Immediate removal day after expansion complete always
Holding expansion months with appliance passive / retainer phase
explanation: Allow bony fill; relapse high if not retained.

q: Crossbite elastics are used for:
options: Correcting individual tooth crossbites with vertical/transverse vectors
explanation: Cross elastics move opposing teeth toward correction.

q: Extraction of premolars in ortho is considered when:
options: Always for every Class I mild 1 mm crowding
Moderate–severe crowding / protrusion needs space and non-extraction plan insufficient
explanation: Extraction decision: crowding, profile, anchorage, Bolton.

q: Maximum anchorage means:
options: Allowing all molars to drift freely mesially always
Minimal unwanted movement of anchor units during space closure
explanation: Anchorage grades: maximum/moderate/minimum — how much anchor loss allowed.

q: Temporary anchorage devices (TADs/miniscrews) provide:
options: Only temporary crowns on implants for dentures
Skeletal anchorage for difficult movements (intrusion, distalization)
Only space maintainers for primary molars
explanation: TADs: absolute anchorage adjuncts.

q: Orthodontic separators are placed to:
explanation: Elastomeric/spring separators open contacts for bands.

q: White spot lesions after braces — prevention includes:
options: Excellent hygiene, fluoride, possible sealants/MI paste protocols
explanation: WSLs = demineralization around plaque-retentive brackets.

q: Debonding residual adhesive ideally removed with:
options: Careful finishing burs/polish protocols minimizing enamel loss
Only ultrasonic scaler on root surface aggressively
explanation: Protect enamel during adhesive cleanup.

q: Phase I (interceptive) ortho goals include:
options: Finish all detailing like full adult braces always in phase I
Only extract all permanent first molars
Correct harmful habits/crossbites/space issues early; reduce Phase II complexity
explanation: Interceptive: limited early goals, not full comprehensive always.

q: Oral habits appliance (crib) is for:
options: Thumb/digit sucking or tongue thrust habit interruption
explanation: Habit-breaking cribs/rakes when counseling insufficient.

q: Supernumerary tooth interfering with eruption — management often:
options: Surgical removal and space/eruption guidance as needed
explanation: Remove obstruction; monitor/align successors.

q: Impacted maxillary canine localization uses:
options: Parallax radiographs / CBCT when indicated for position and pathology
explanation: Locate canine bucco-palatally before surgical-ortho exposure.

q: Open exposure vs closed eruption technique for impacted canine depends on:
options: Always only closed for all cases without exception
Position of tooth, keratinized tissue needs, and ortho plan
explanation: Surgical exposure technique individualized.

q: Space regainer differs from space maintainer because it:
options: Actively reopens lost space rather than only holding existing space
explanation: Regainer = active; maintainer = passive.

q: Lip bumper is used mainly to:
options: Expand midpalatal suture rapidly in adults
Reduce lower lip pressure on incisors / upright molars distal slightly / preserve E-space
explanation: Lip bumper: soft tissue shield + molar uprighting effects.

q: Class II elastics typically run:
options: From upper anterior/canine region to lower molar region
Only on retainers at night without brackets ever
Only across midline diastema without AP vector
From lower molar to upper molar only vertical box always
explanation: Class II elastics: anterior maxilla → posterior mandible vector.

q: Class III elastics run:
options: From upper canine to lower molar only (that's Class II)
From lower anterior to upper molar region
Only vertical triangular on one tooth
explanation: Class III elastics opposite vector to Class II.

q: Over-retained primary tooth with permanent successor present and path clear — often:
options: Always root canal primary and keep forever
Extract primary to allow permanent eruption
explanation: Remove over-retained primary when successor ready.

q: Congenitally missing maxillary lateral — treatment options include:
options: Only observation without any plan ever
Canine substitution or space opening for prosthetic replacement (implant/bridge) — case planned
explanation: Interdisciplinary: ortho space open vs substitute.

q: Root canal treated primary molar that is restorable still often receives:
options: Only temporary zinc oxide without coverage long term
Only orthodontic band without occlusal coverage always
explanation: After pulpectomy, full coverage preferred.

q: Glass ionomer in pedo is valued for:
options: Chemical adhesion and fluoride release in cervical/ART contexts
Best for high-stress permanent cusp replacement only always
explanation: GI: adhesion + F release; useful in pedo/ART.

q: Midazolam oral sedation overdose signs include:
options: Excessive CNS/respiratory depression — reverse with flumazenil and support airway
Immediate hyperthermia only like MH always
explanation: Benzodiazepine OD: airway + flumazenil (with seizure caution in chronic benzo users).

q: Uncooperative child needing extensive dental work with failed behavior guidance — may require:
options: Discharge without any plan or referral
Deep sedation / GA in appropriate facility with informed consent
explanation: Stepwise: basic guidance → N2O → sedation/GA referral when indicated.

q: Primary herpetic gingivostomatitis in a child — dental elective care?
options: Defer elective care until resolved; supportive management
Scale and polish aggressively same day always
explanation: Acute viral infection: postpone elective dentistry.
